coredata.dc:title: Environmentally-friendly functionalized carbon dots for the detection of metronidazole and antibacterial applications

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinyu
Yang, Longyan
Zhang, Yi
Yin, Pengfei
Sun, Xiyuan
coredata.dc:description: The irrational use of metronidazole (MNZ) poses a significant risk to human health, hence quantitative detection is critical. In this study, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) were synthesized from orange peel and ethylenediamine (EDA) by a one-step hydrothermal method to construct a fluorescent sensing strategy for detecting MNZ. The synthesized CQDs-NH2 showed high sensitivity and selectivity for MNZ, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.19 μM and a linear range of 0–240 μM. The detection mechanism was investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption fluorescence spectroscopy, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and fluorescence decay spectroscopy analysis. It was revealed that the detection mechanism of CQDs-NH2 is based on a synergistic effect of the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Moreover, a composite film developed using CQDs-NH2 demonstrated effective UV-blocking capabilities and significant antibacterial effects on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Fluorescent sensor
Metronidazole
DFT
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metronidazole in real samples
Preparation and evaluation of the antibacterial activity of CQDs-NH2/PVA composite film
Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism
Detection of metronidazole in real samples
Preparation and antibacterial application of composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (b) particle size distribution histogram, (c) XRD image, (d) FTIR spectrum of CQDs-NH2, (e) XPS spectra of CQDs-NH2 and high-resolution peaks of (f) C1s, (g) N1s and (h) O1s.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CQDs-NH2 solution. Insets: photographs of CQDs-NH2 solution under natural light (I) and 365 nm UV light (II). (b) Emission spectra of CQDs-NH2 upon excitation at different wavelengths.

(a-b) Selectivity of CQDs-NH2 for metal ions, antibiotics and amino acids, (c) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-NH2 upon addition of diverse concentrations of MNZ (0–400 μM), (d) Stern-Volmer calibration curves, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2, F is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 after the addition of MNZ, (e) Absorption spectra of MNZ and excitation and emission spectra of CQDs-NH2, (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs-NH2, MNZ and the mixture of both, (g) Fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs-NH2 solution before and after the addition of MNZ.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) transmittance spectrum of prepared composite films, (c) Digital image of prepared composite films under visible and UV light (365 nm): (i) neat PVA film, (ii) 1 %CP, (iii) 2 %CP, (iv) 3 %CP, (v) 4 %CP and (vi) 5 %CP composite films, (d) Effect of the prepared composite films on the growth of E. coli and S. aureus.
(a) The preparation process of CQDs-NH2, (b) schematic illustration for the detection of metronidazole and (c) preparation of PVA-based composite antibacterial film.

head.ce:title: Environmentally-friendly functionalized carbon dots for the detection of metronidazole and antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’an 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’an 625014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The irrational use of metronidazole (MNZ) poses a significant risk to human health, hence quantitative detection is critical. In this study, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) were synthesized from orange peel and ethylenediamine (EDA) by a one-step hydrothermal method to construct a fluorescent sensing strategy for detecting MNZ. The synthesized CQDs-NH2 showed high sensitivity and selectivity for MNZ, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.19 μM and a linear range of 0–240 μM. The detection mechanism was investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption fluorescence spectroscopy, density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and fluorescence decay spectroscopy analysis. It was revealed that the detection mechanism of CQDs-NH2 is based on a synergistic effect of the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Moreover, a composite film developed using CQDs-NH2 demonstrated effective UV-blocking capabilities and significant antibacterial effects on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole (MNZ) is an effective antibiotic primarily used to treat anaerobic infections as well as various bacterial infections in both humans and animals. It is also used as a feed additive to promote growth and eliminate parasites [1]. However, MNZ has been linked to several harmful side effects, including carcinogenic [2], mutagenic [3], and genotoxic [4] properties. Misuse or irrational use of MNZ can lead to a range of toxic reactions such as spasticity, peripheral neuropathy, and ataxia [5], which pose serious risks to human health. Therefore, to protect human health and ensure food safety, it is essential to develop a simple, rapid, highly selective, and sensitive method for detecting MNZ. Currently, various established methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography [6], electrochemical methods [7], and spectrophotometric methods [8] have been employed for MNZ detection. However, these methods often face challenges, including complex and bulky equipment, high operating costs, and lengthy detection times.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are innovative zero-dimensional nanomaterials that exhibit several remarkable properties, including photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and strong antibacterial capabilities. These features open up a range of potential applications across various fields. Recently, fluorescent sensors based on CQDs have attracted much attention due to their ease of use, low cost, and rapid response times [9], and they are widely used to detect various substances. Notably, the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs offer a unique advantage in identifying key molecules or drug residues within organisms. For instance, Jin et al. developed nitrogen-doped CQDs that demonstrated excellent performance in detecting iron ions (Fe3+) [10]. Hu et al. synthesized highly fluorescent blue CQDs for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics in environmental water, which showed high selectivity and sensitivity [11]. Ren et al. created CQDs from organic compounds for the rapid determination of MNZ with a detection limit of 0.22 μM [12].

Biomass-derived CQDs are considered a promising alternative to chemically synthesized CQDs due to their environmentally friendly and cost-effective production methods. It has been reported that citrus fruits are produced and sold in large quantities, with their peels containing a high amount of carbohydrates, making them a valuable carbon source for the fabrication of CQDs [13]. In light of this, the present study constructed a biomass carbon-based fluorescent sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity for MNZ using honey orange peel. In addition, CQDs exhibit significant antibacterial properties due to their unique surface chemistry. Composite films made from CQDs have garnered considerable attention because of their excellent optical and antibacterial characteristics. For instance, Chen et al. demonstrated that films coated with synthesized CQDs achieved an antimicrobial activity of up to 90 % [14]. Lin et al. developed a composite film incorporating chitosan and nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs), which not only displayed good optical and UV-blocking properties but also served as an effective antibacterial agent for food packaging [15]. By incorporating CQDs, these films gain enhanced functionalities, thereby expanding their potential applications.

In this study, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) were synthesized efficiently and environmentally using a one-step hydrothermal method, utilizing orange peel as the carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA) as the nitrogen source. These CQDs-NH2 were developed for the detection of MNZ and for creating composite antibacterial films (see Scheme 1 ). The synthesized CQDs-NH2 demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity as a fluorescent sensor for MNZ detection, and they were successfully employed for the accurate determination and analysis of MNZ in real samples. Additionally, the interaction between CQDs-NH2 and MNZ was examined through density functional theory (DFT) calculations, allowing for an exploration of the sensing mechanism. Moreover, the synthesized CQDs-NH2 were integrated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, resulting in a composite material that showed significant antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). This work successfully establishes a reliable and sensitive method for detecting MNZ and demonstrates the use of CQDs-NH2 in creating PVA-based composite films with antibacterial properties, broadening their potential applications.

Honey oranges were purchased from a supermarket in Ya'an City, Sichuan Province, and peel waste was collected. MNZ (C6H9N3O3) and EDA (C2H8N2) were purchased from Chengdu Haobao You Technology Co. The reagents of FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl2·2H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, AlCl3·6H2O, NH4Cl, CuCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, KCl, ZnCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, LiCl, Pb(NO3)2, SnCl2·2H2O, CrCl3, CdCl2, H3BO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, and NaOH were provided by Chengdu Kelong Reagent Co. Amino acids including alanine, L-glutamic acid, methionine, tryptophan, arginine, histidine, threonine, serine, lysine, tyrosine, phenylalanine, L-aspartic acid, glycine and four different antibiotics (Kanamycin Monsulfate, Erythromycin, Florfenicol, and 2-Methylimidazole) were purchased from McLean Biochemicals (Shanghai) Co. PVA was purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co. Ltd. Nutrient agar and nutrient broth were purchased from Qingdao Haibo Biotechnology Co. E. coli and S. aureus were kindly provided by the College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University. All chemicals were utilized in their received state, were of analytical grade, and did not require further purification steps. Deionized water was uniformly used in performing all experiments. The pH of the experimental solutions was adjusted using Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (0.04 mol/L of H3BO3, H3PO4 and CH3COOH prepared and used with 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution).

The fluorescence spectra of the samples were analyzed using an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-1600 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The average particle size of the CQDs-NH2 was determined through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging conducted with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) and analyzed using ImageJ software. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained from a DX-2700B X-ray diffractometer in Dandong, China. Structural and functional group analyses were performed with a Nicolet Is20 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer instrument (Thermos Inc., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded and the elements of CQDs-NH2 were probed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Quantum yields and fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-NH2 were measured using an FLS980 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

The CQDs-NH2 was synthesized using a hydrothermal method with honey orange peel serving as the carbon precursor and EDA as the nitrogen precursor. The process began with cleaning and drying the orange peels, which were then ground into a fine powder. Next, the mixture of the orange peel powder (1 g) and EDA (200 μL) was moved into a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 220 ℃ for 24 h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature spontaneously. The crude solution was filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous filter and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, a portion of the brown-yellow solution was dried in an oven at 60 ℃ to obtain a brown solid powder for further characterization. While the resultant CQDs-NH2 solution was kept in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future use.

Fig. S1 illustrates the effects of reaction temperature, time, and EDA dosage on the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CQDs-NH2. Due to the limited high-temperature resistance of the reactor (maximum of 220 ℃), the hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 220 ℃. The optimal conditions were established with a reaction time of 24 h. and an EDA amount of 200 µL, under which the fluorescence intensity of the resulting CQDs-NH2 solution reached its peak value.

To detect MNZ more accurately, the effects of reaction time and pH on the sensing process were investigated (Refer to Fig. S2 in the Supplementary material). Under optimal detection conditions, 0.04 g of MNZ was dissolved in 40 mL of water to obtain a stock solution of MNZ at a concentration of 6 mmol/L. Then, different volumes of MNZ solution were added to 20 μL of CQDs-NH2 solution, with a total solution volume of 3 mL. The final concentration of MNZ was between 0 and 400 μM. The intensity of the fluorescence emission spectra was then measured at an excitation slit of 15 nm and an emission slit of 6.5 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. For the blank control solution, 20 μL of CQDs-NH2 solution was added to the centrifuge tube, followed by making up the remaining volume with water to 3 mL. In addition, the effects of various metal cations, antibiotics and amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 were explored to investigate the selectivity of CQDs-NH2 for MNZ further. Three parallel experiments were performed for each test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metronidazole in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the usefulness of the biomass sensor in real environments, the standard addition method was utilized to detect MNZ in various samples. Tap water samples were collected from the laboratory at Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'an Campus, while river water samples were gathered from the Qingyi River in the Yucheng District of Ya'an City. Additionally, Milk and honey were purchased from a local supermarket. Before analysis, the water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous filter to remove large suspended particles. The milk and honey samples were diluted by a factor of 200. Each test was conducted in triplicate to ensure accuracy.

The Karaboga [16] artificial bee colony algorithm was used to obtain the initial structures of metronidazole and functionalized carbon quantum dot complexes by the ABCluster 2.0 program [17]. The structures of the complexes obtained by ABCluster were optimized by performing DFT calculations using the ORCA program [18]. All calculations were performed by describing the interactions using the B3LYP functional and the def2-SVP basis set, as well as by employing the DFT-D3 correction proposed by Grimme [19]. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis were carried out by the Multiwfn program [20] to reveal interaction characteristics. The basis set superposition errors (BSSE) adsorption energy was corrected in the calculations [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and evaluation of the antibacterial activity of CQDs-NH2/PVA composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The PVA films were prepared using a solvent-casting method. 6 g of PVA were mixed with 100 mL of deionized water, then heated and stirred at 95 ℃ for half an hour until the mixture became clear and transparent. The CQDs-NH2 solution was added to the PVA solution, ensuring thorough mixing. The final concentrations of CQDs-NH2 were 0 %, 1 %, 2 %, 3 %, 4 %, and 5 %, respectively. The mixed solution was poured into a membrane apparatus and dried at room temperature to obtain composite films, which were labeled as 0 %CP, 1 %CP, 2 %CP, 3 %CP, 4 %CP, and 5 %CP, respectively.

For testing the antibacterial properties of the films, E. coli (Gram-negative bacterium) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterium) were selected due to their common presence in various environments. The strains were inoculated in LB broth medium and after incubation at 37 ℃, the prepared bacterial solution was diluted to 10–7 times in a gradient. The film-forming solution was mixed homogeneously with the bacterial solution, and 100 μL of this mixture was uniformly coated on an agar plate and incubated at 37 ℃ for 18–36 h. The antibacterial ability of the CQDs-NH2/PVA composite film can be assessed using the plate colony counting method under various conditions. Moreover, the UV-blocking ability and water contact angle(WCA) of the composite films were tested.

The microscopic particle morphology and particle size distribution of CQDs-NH2 were measured by TEM. As can be observed in Fig. 1 (a), the appearance of CQDs-NH2 is nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed without agglomeration. The particle size range was within 3–7 nm with an average size of 4.52 nm, as indicated in Fig. 1(b). The inset HRTEM image reveals distinct lattice stripes, demonstrating a well-defined lattice structure. The measured lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon [22]. The XRD pattern presented in Fig. 1(c) shows a broad diffraction peak at 24.2°, which indicates that the peak arises from the diffraction of disordered carbon, confirming that the synthesized CQDs-NH2 is amorphous [23]. The functional groups of CQDs-NH2 were distinguished by FTIR spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 1(d). It was found that a segment of a broad absorption band was observed in the range of 3392 cm−1 to 3284 cm−1 of the spectrum, which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the two functional groups, O-H (3392 cm−1) and NH2 (3284 cm−1), respectively [24,25]. The peak at 2942 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-H bonds [26]. The absorption band at 1635 cm−1 is attributed to C=O groups [27] and the peak at 1570 cm−1 reflects the stretching vibration of the C=C bond [28]. Absorption peaks near 1360 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 are associated with C-OH and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively [26,29]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1440 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond [30].

In order to deeply investigate the chemical composition and elemental distribution properties of CQDs-NH2, it was characterized in detail by the XPS technique. In the full-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1(e)), it can be observed that three main peaks are displayed at 284, 399, and 532 eV, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. It is revealed that CQDs-NH2 mainly contain three elements, C, N, and O, and their relative atomic percentages are 76 %, 11.3 %, and 12.7 %. The corresponding individual components were further analyzed in detail by high-resolution energy spectroscopy. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 1(f)) showed three different carbon signals, with binding energy peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 287.9 eV corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-O, and C=O, respectively [31]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) indicated that there were three peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.7 eV, and 401.0 eV are linked to the (C)3-N, O=C-NH, and C-NH2 bonds [32]. The fitted peaks at 530.9 eV, 533.0 eV, and 531.9 eV were observed in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1(h)) belonging to O-C=O, C-OH/C-O-C, and C=O, respectively [33]. The XPS patterns indicated that the synthesized CQDs-NH2 contained nitrogenous moiety –NH2 and oxygenated functional groups −C=O- and –OH. Both XPS patterns and FTIR analysis results indicated the successful construction of CQDs-NH2, and the prepared CQDs-NH2 surface was rich in hydrophilic groups and had good water solubility, which provided favorable conditions for subsequent in-depth studies.

The optical properties of the CQDs-NH2 were investigated by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, with the relevant spectra illustrated in Fig. 2 . As presented in Fig. 2(a), CQDs-NH2 showed two absorption bands at 275 and 339 nm, which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of the SP2 hybridized carbon atom domains in graphite and the n-π* transition induced by the surface functional groups, respectively [34,35]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra showed that the optimal excitation wavelength was 350 nm, and its optimal emission peak was located at 430 nm. The insets in Fig. 2(a) exhibited that the homogeneously dispersed CQDs-NH2 solution appeared pale yellow under natural light irradiation (left) and bright blue under UV light (right), further visually exhibiting their blue fluorescence character.

The effects of different excitation wavelengths on the photoluminescence of CQDs-NH2 were detailed in Fig. 2(b). As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence emission peak of CQDs-NH2 showed an initial increase followed by a decrease. This excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior may be attributed to the diversity of their sizes and the variability of their surface states [36]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized CQDs-NH2 was determined to be 4.97 %, which is comparable to other values mentioned in the literature for CQDs prepared from biomass by-products [37,38].

It is essential to ensure the fluorescence stability of CQDs-NH2 under various conditions. Therefore, the effects on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) of CQDs-NH2 under different concentrations of NaCl solution, UV irradiation time, storage time and incubation temperature conditions were investigated. F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2, F is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 after the addition of MNZ. As shown in Fig. S3(a), the fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged with the gradual increase in NaCl solution concentration, maintaining consistent results even at a concentration of 2.4 mM. In Fig. S3(b), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 exhibited only slight fluctuations and remained stable when subjected to UV irradiation for 0 to 160 min. For storage studies, CQDs-NH2 were kept at 4℃, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded every 10 days. The results (Fig. S3(c)) demonstrated good photostability over time. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. S3(d), the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 stayed relatively stable when the temperature was increased from 15 ℃ to 40 ℃, indicating robust fluorescence stability. These findings highlight the unique fluorescence properties of CQDs-NH2, suggesting their potential use as fluorescent probes across various fields.

The detection conditions of MNZ were optimized. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MNZ on CQDs-NH2 reached the maximum value at 1 min of reaction (see Fig. S2a), and there was no significant change in the quenching effect by continuing the reaction. As shown in Fig. S2b, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 solution (F0) had the maximum fluorescence intensity at pH 7. Its effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 in the presence of MNZ reaches its maximum at pH 7, the condition under which the quenching effect is best. Therefore, MNZ was detected under the best reaction conditions of an incubation time of 1 min and a pH of 7.

Additionally, to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor, changes in fluorescence intensity in the presence of different kinds of metal ions, antibiotics and amino acid molecules were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 (a) when CQDs-NH2 reacts with metal ions and antibiotics of the same concentration (240 μM), with MNZ having the most pronounced bursting effect on it, with a quenching rate of 69.8 %. Good selectivity of carbon dots for ferric ions has been reported in the literature [39], but their selectivity in this method may vary somewhat due to factors such as carbon dots being synthesized from biomass materials, or different concentrations in the system. At the same concentration, amino acid molecules had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2, and the quenching effect was not obvious (Fig. 3(b)). The above results indicated that CQDs-NH2 exhibits superior selectivity for the detection of MNZ over other competing ions, antibiotics and small molecule compounds. A sensitivity test was conducted to assess the response of the FL signals before and after exposure to varying concentrations of MNZ ranging from 0 to 400 μM (Fig. 3(c)). The outcomes suggest that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-NH2 quenches proportionally as the concentration of MNZ increases. The relationship can be fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation [40], and the linear relationship is expressed as follows:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm in the absence and presence of MNZ, respectively, KSV is the quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher MNZ.

The results showed that the CQDs-NH2 fluorescent sensor exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of MNZ in the range of 0–240 μM (see Fig. 3(d)). The linear equation obtained by fitting with this linear relationship was F0/F = 0.0105CMNZ + 0.9594, R2 = 0.9908, and a limit of detection (LOD) was 0.19 μM. In addition, compared with the previously reported methods for the detection of MNZ by CQDs fluorescent sensors (Table 1 ), the technique had a lower limit of detection and a wider range of detection, which indicated that the prepared CQDs-NH2 has high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 3(e), it is evident that there is a high degree of overlap between the excitation peaks of CQDs-NH2 and the absorption peaks of MNZ. This observation suggests that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon may mainly originate from the inner filter effect (IFE). After adding MNZ to the CQDs-NH2 solution, the original absorption peak of CQDs-NH2 shows a significant blue shift at 339 nm, while the characteristic absorption peak of MNZ appears around 319 nm (Fig. 3(f)). This change not only indicates the interaction between the two substances but also implies the possible formation of a non-luminescent complex between them. To further explore the potential quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs-NH2 solutions in the presence and absence of MNZ were measured. The biexponential curve-fitting analysis of the fluorescence decay spectra is shown in Fig. 3(g). The average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-NH2 alone was 2.91 ns, while the average fluorescence lifetime after the addition of MNZ was 2.86 ns, showing no significant change. This result supports static quenching as the main mechanism for the effect of MNZ on the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2. In conclusion, the interaction between carbon dots and MNZ is characterized by a combination of static quenching and inner filter effects.

To further understand the sensing mechanism, the MNZ@CQDs complexes were investigated using DFT calculations. The molecular structure of MNZ was previously optimized in our group’s earlier work [45]. The carbon cluster model of C54H18 was employed to represent CQDs, which has been successfully applied in recent studies [46–48]. Based on the infrared spectrograms of CQDs-NH2, functionalized carbon quantum dots (f-CQDs) were constructed by modifying the edges of the CQDs with key functional groups including hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2), and carboxyl (–COOH) groups. Fig. S4(a) shows the optimized configurations of MNZ on the f-CQDs surface with different orientations. The most stable configuration of MNZ@CQDs and MNZ@f-CQDs were given in Fig. S4(b) and Fig. S4(c). It can be seen from Fig. S4 (c) that hydrogen bonds were formed, with the shortest adsorption distance of 2.38 Å between the O atom of the –NO2 on MNZ and the H atom of the –NH2 on f-CQDs. Meanwhile, the HOMO and LUMO of the most stable complexes MNZ@CQDs and MNZ@f-CQDs were obtained (Fig. S4(d)). The optimized configurations show that their energy gaps (Eg) values are 2.56 and 2.47 eV, respectively. This difference arises from the incorporation of the –NH2 functional group, which increases the degree of surface oxidation and the number of defects on the CQDs, leading to a decrease in the (Eg) value [49]. Moreover, the adsorption energy (Eads) of MNZ on CQDs-NH2 is much larger at −1.04 eV (see Table S1), which makes it easier to form stable complexes[50], which is why experimentally we introduced –NH2. MEP image (Fig. S4(e-h)) shows that the –NO2 group of MNZ exhibits a high negative electron density (red) and is suitable for nucleophilic attack. The –NH2 is the electrophilic attack site of f-CQDs with high positive electron density (blue).

QTAIM is often used to characterize binding interactions. Among them, the electron density at bond critical points (BCPs) indicates the interaction strength, while the BCPs and bond paths (see Fig. S5) demonstrate the existence of interaction. Relevant topological parameters such as charge density ρ(r), Laplace of charge density (∇2 ρ), potential energy density V(r), kinetic energy density G(r), and energy density H(r) are obtained by calculating the interaction critical point, and the topological parameters of BCPs are summarized in Table S1. It is clear that the O…H interaction critical point has the highest electron density values of 0.012 and 0.036 a.u, indicating a stronger interaction [51]. The values of ∇2 ρ and H(r) reported in Table S1 are both positive (∇2 ρ > 0 and H(r) > 0), indicating the predominance of closed-shell interactions between MNZ and CQDs-NH2 [52]. Based on these findings, the sensing mechanism between MNZ and CQDs-NH2 may be realized mainly from the interaction between nucleophilic (containing oxygen atom) and electrophilic (–NH2) groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metronidazole in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the significance and applicability of the CQDs-NH2 biomass sensor in real liquid samples, it was applied to the detection of MNZ in water and liquid food samples. The spiked recovery experimental method was employed by adding different concentrations of MNZ to four treated samples, with the results summarized in Table S2. The recovery rates for the samples ranged from 97.55 % to 102.67 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) consistently remained below 4.64 % (n = 3). These results demonstrated that the synthesized CQDs-NH2 is feasible for detecting MNZ in a real environment, and it has a broad potential for practical application. The method proves to be highly accurate, offering a simple and reliable approach for the detection of MNZ in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and antibacterial application of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Films created by incorporating orange peel-derived carbon quantum dots into PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) exhibit excellent optical, UV-blocking, and bacteriostatic properties, making them promising candidates for development as active packaging materials.

A UV–visible spectrophotometer (200–800 nm) was used to evaluate the UV-blocking ability of the composite films. The films were cut into suitable lengths and placed in a quartz test tube for wavelength scanning, allowing for the recording of their absorption and transmission spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the pure PVA film displayed a small absorption peak in the 200–400 nm range. However, the absorption value of the composite films gradually increased with the increase of CQDs-NH2 concentration, indicating the successful doping of CQDs-NH2 and strong absorption properties in this wavelength range, and the good dispersion and compatibility of CQDs-NH2 in the PVA matrix. Meanwhile, the test results of the light transmission properties of the composite films are displayed in Fig. 4(b). The transmittance of the composite films decreased as the concentration of CQDs-NH2 increased. Notably, the maximum transmittance of the 5 % CP composite film in the UV region dropped to 39.7 %, indicating that the composite film achieved a maximum UV blocking rate of 60.3 %, thereby demonstrating effective UV-blocking performance. The changes in the optical properties of the composite film may originate from the hydrogen bonding between the CQDs-NH2 and the polymer chains [53]. In addition, the water contact angle of the composite films indicate their good hydrophilicity (see Fig. S6). Under irradiation with 365 nm UV light, the composite film exhibited good blue light emission characteristics (Fig. 4(c)).

Due to the strong antibacterial activity of the prepared CDs against E. coli and S. aureus, the antibacterial ability of the composite film was studied by the plate colony counting method. The experiment result showed (Fig. 4(d)) that the pure PVA film can not inhibit bacterial growth, and the increase in the concentration of CQDS-NH2 led to a significant improvement in the inhibitory effect of the composite film. The improved bacteriostatic performance of the composite films can be attributed to the unique properties of the active filler CQDs-NH2 [54]. Among them, 5 % CP had a better inhibitory effect, and the inhibitory activity against S. aureus reached about 90 %. The difference in the inhibitory activity may be related to the variability in the structure of the cell wall of the bacterial species. The monolayer membrane structure of Gram-positive bacteria contains a negatively charged phosphomimetic acid component, which interacts with the amino groups on the surface of the carbon dots, destroying the structural integrity of the bacterial surface, triggering cell membrane damage and leakage of the contents, leading to bacterial death [55]. The outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria is rich in lipopolysaccharides, which are attached to positively charged bonds making the bacterial binding to carbon dots relatively weak [55,56].

In summary, a CQDs-NH2 fluorescent sensor for the detection of MNZ was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using orange peel and EDA as raw materials. The prepared CQDs-NH2 were characterized using TEM, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, fluorescence spectroscopy, and fluorescence lifetime. The CQDs-NH2 emits blue light and has excellent stability, with high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of MNZ in the range of 0–240 μM, and the LOD was 0.19 μM. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, DFT calculations, and fluorescence decay spectroscopy analysis were used to investigate the detection mechanism. Results indicated that the detection mechanism of MNZ by CQDs-NH2 can be based on the interaction between the two substances, which is a synergistic effect of IFE and static quenching. Furthermore, tests conducted with real samples showed good accuracy, with recoveries ranging from 97.55 % to 102.67 %. Additionally, composite films prepared from CQDs-NH2 combined with PVA exhibited enhanced UV-blocking and antimicrobial properties. Therefore, the biomass fluorescence sensor developed in this study offers a novel method for detecting MNZ in real samples, expands the applications for composite films, and holds significant promise for future use.

Xinyu Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Longyan Yang: Formal analysis. Yi Zhang: Investigation. Pengfei Yin: Validation. Xiyuan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12404304).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113985.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of carbon dots with bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission: fast gram-type identification and selective Gram-positive bacterial inactivation
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yang, G. Gao, X. Zhang, Y. Ma, X. Chen, F. Wu, One-step synthesis of carbon dots with bacterial contact-enhanced fluorescence emission: fast gram-type identification and selective Gram-positive bacterial inactivation, Carbon, 2019, 146: 827-839.
